the_signal.headline: Agent memory architecture now separates scaling from reliability
the_signal.body: n8n's breakdown of in-context buffers vs. vector stores exposes a hard choice: speed or persistence. GTM engineers building multi-turn revenue workflows need to decide now whether stateless agents (faster, cheaper) or persistent memory (reliable, data-heavy) fit their playbook. This isn't theoretical—it directly impacts whether your deal qualification agent remembers context across 10 touchpoints or forgets after conversation reset.

tool_drop.name: n8n's AI Agent Memory Guide
tool_drop.what: Architectural patterns for implementing persistent memory into LLM agents without token bleed or latency penalties.
tool_drop.why: You're probably shipping agents with in-context memory only—this shows the production path to remember lead history, objection handling, and deal stage across sessions.

thread_of_day.title: Claude Code vs. n8n: Five questions to pick your agent stack
thread_of_day.takeaway: The real split isn't tool vs. platform—it's whether you own compliance, observability, and retry logic (n8n) or bet on Claude's LLM-first UX (Claude Code).

quote.text: Context engineering for LLMs goes beyond prompt design. You need to manage context rot, budget tokens, and build reliable production AI agents.
quote.source: n8n Blog: Fix Agent Failures With Context Engineering for LLMs

whats_moving.context: Vector stores and persistent layers are moving from nice-to-have to required for multi-turn revenue conversations that don't reset context mid-deal.
Automated detection and response workflows are shipping for prompt injection and data exfiltration risks—table stakes for agents accessing Salesforce or deal data.
RAG + threat intelligence patterns are now being applied to revenue ops workflows, not just SOC—expect agent-assisted qualification to flag red flags in real-time.

act_on_this: Map your current agent's memory footprint: does it persist lead context, objection history, or deal stage across sessions? If not, prototype a vector store integration this week—your qualification accuracy will plateau without it.

numbers.stat: 75+ capabilities expected from modern agent development tools according to n8n's capability audit
numbers.context: Most teams ship with 20–30% of that scope, which means your agent's failure modes (identity drift, intent misclassification, tool hallucination) are probably preventable with architecture you're not using yet.
